# Gravity waves generated by sounds from big bang phase transitions

> Inhomogeneities associated with the cosmological QCD and electroweak phase transitions produce hydrodynamical perturbations, longitudinal sounds and rotations. It has been demonstrated by Hindmarsh et al. that the sounds produce gravity waves (GW) well after the phase transition is over. We further argue that, under certain conditions, an inverse acoustic cascade may occur and move sound perturbations from the (UV) momentum scale at which the sound is originally produced to much smaller (IR) momenta. The weak turbulence regime of this cascade is studied via the Boltzmann equation, possessing stationary power and time-dependent self-similar solutions. We suggest certain indices for the strong turbulence regime as well, into which the cascade eventually proceeds. Finally, we point out that two on-shell sound waves can produce one on-shell gravity wave, and we evaluate the rate of the process using a standard sound loop diagram.

## Key claim

Under certain conditions, an inverse acoustic cascade may move sound perturbations from the UV momentum scale at which sound is originally produced to much smaller IR momenta, potentially amplifying the gravity wave signal from cosmological QCD and electroweak phase transitions by a huge factor; the authors suggest specific indices for the strong turbulence regime into which the cascade eventually proceeds, but note that dedicated theoretical studies and numerical simulations are required to verify these indices.

## Method

The authors study the weak turbulence regime of the inverse acoustic cascade via the Boltzmann equation, possessing stationary power and time-dependent self-similar solutions. They evaluate the rate of the two-on-shell-sound-waves-to-one-on-shell-gravity-wave process using a standard sound loop diagram, and they suggest indices for the strong turbulence regime by analogy with scalar and gluon cascades, applying scaling analysis and Katz-Kontorovich transformations to the kinetic equations.

## Key numbers

- Reynolds number for the early Universe at QCD phase transition: Re_EU ~ 10^19 (compared to Re_RHIC ~ 48π)
- QCD transition temperature: T_QCD = 170 MeV; electroweak transition temperature: T_EW ~ 100 GeV
- Redshift of QCD transition: z_QCD = 7.6 × 10^11; redshift of electroweak transition: z_EW ~ 4 × 10^14
- Time of QCD phase transition: t_QCD = 4 × 10^-5 s; time of electroweak phase transition: t_EW ~ 10^-11 s
- Current bound on GW energy density: Ω_GW(f = 2.8 nHz) · (h_0/0.73)^2 < 1.3 × 10^-9
- Stationary power solution index for decay scenario (binary decays allowed): s_decay = 9/2
- Stationary power solution index for non-decay scenario (four-wave interactions): s_nondecay = 10/3
- Suggested strong turbulence index (leading): s_strong = 4; suggested strong turbulence index (subleading): s_strong = 6

## Context

This work extends the findings of Hindmarsh et al. (2013), who demonstrated that sounds from cosmological phase transitions produce gravity waves well after the phase transition is over, and who performed numerical simulations of the electroweak phase transition. The paper also builds on the foundational work of Witten (1984) on bubble dynamics and gravity wave production from QCD phase transitions, and on Hogan (1986) who first identified relevant frequencies and provided initial estimates of radiation intensity. The inverse acoustic cascade concept enables a multi-stage theoretical framework for understanding how sound perturbations can be focused toward small momenta, potentially making gravity wave signals from the QCD transition observable by pulsar timing arrays such as the North American Nanohertz Observatory for Gravitational Radiation, European Pulsar Timing Array, and Parkes Pulsar Timing Array.
